docs.title: Boundaries and Responsibilities
docs.text: Actor / System Owns Does not own Chakra Commerce Platform Order, Inventory, Customer domains Payment processing, physical fulfillment Payment Gateway Card authorization and fraud Order state, inventory Warehouse System Physical fulfillment execution Stock levels (reads from Inventory via event) Grafana Stack Observability storage and alerting Application logic, deployment ArgoCD Deployment reconciliation Service implementation, config authoring Domain Engineer Contract correctness Implementation volume Platform Operator Runtime SLA compliance Contract authoring

docs.title: Contracts
docs.text:
contracts/ is the only directory in the repository that humans author directly. Every other artifact — service code, Helm charts, Prometheus alerts, CI pipelines — is either derived from these files by a script or generated by an AI agent given these files as input.

This is the machine-readable statement of what the system must do. Implementation is downstream of it.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
contracts/
├── slas/                      # Numeric SLA targets (availability, latency, throughput)
├── domain-invariants/         # Business rules that can never be violated
└── event-schemas/             # Canonical JSON Schemas for all domain events

docs.title: SLA Targets
docs.text:
Each service has a YAML file in contracts/slas/ that defines the numeric promises the service makes. These flow downstream via a deterministic script to:

  • observability/slos/ — measurement windows and error budgets
  • observability/alerts/ — PrometheusRule burn rate alerts

The key property: a target is written once and only once. The validate-contracts.sh script checks that every SLA file has a matching SLO file and a matching alert file. Drift is caught in CI.

SLA file Service Key targets orders-sla.yaml Orders 99.95% availability, 500ms p99, 5s saga compensation max inventory-sla.yaml Inventory 99.9% availability, 100ms read p99, 0 oversell tolerance customers-sla.yaml Customers 99.9% availability, 300ms p99, 30-day GDPR deletion platform-sla.yaml Platform EKS 99.95%, RTO 4h, RPO 15min fulfillment-sla.yaml Fulfillment Gateway 99.9% dispatch success, 2s WMS p99, circuit breaker thresholds

docs.title: Domain Invariants
docs.text:
Business rules that the system must never violate, expressed in plain language and numbered for traceability. Each invariant ID (INV-ORD-001, INV-INV-003, etc.) maps to:

  • A named test in services/*/tests/domain/
  • A guard in the aggregate root or command handler
  • A compliance check in the architectural review (ai-agents/tasks/agent/architectural-compliance-review.md)
Invariants file Service Key invariants orders-invariants.md Orders 10 invariants: no empty orders, valid transitions, stock before payment, immutable after confirmation inventory-invariants.md Inventory 6 invariants: no oversell, no over-release, Redis not authoritative for writes customers-invariants.md Customers 5 invariants: unique emails, suspended customer blocks orders, cascading deletion

docs.title: Event Schemas
docs.text:
Canonical JSON Schemas (Draft-07) for all domain events. These are the published language of the system — the format that every producer must emit and every consumer must accept.

Each schema has a matching TypeScript interface in services/*/src/domain/events/. The validate-contracts.sh script checks that the TypeScript class exists for every schema file.

Schema Topic Owner OrderPlaced.json chakra.orders.placed orders-team OrderCancelled.json chakra.orders.cancelled orders-team StockReserved.json chakra.inventory.reserved inventory-team CustomerRegistered.json chakra.customers.registered customers-team

docs.title: Validation in CI
docs.text:
The validate-contracts.sh script runs on every push and checks:

  1. Every contracts/slas/*.yaml has a matching observability/slos/*.yaml
  2. Every contracts/slas/*.yaml has a matching observability/alerts/*-burnrate.yaml
  3. Every contracts/event-schemas/*.json has a matching TypeScript event class in services/
  4. Every ADR file appears in docs/adrs/README.md
# Run locally
bash tooling/validate-contracts.sh

All 20 checks currently pass. See ADR-0001 for the contract-first development decision.

docs.title: Orders Domain Invariants
docs.text:
These invariants are the non-negotiable business rules for the Orders bounded context. Each invariant has a unique ID that maps directly to a named test in services/orders/tests/domain/ and a guard in the aggregate root.

An implementation that does not enforce all invariants below is not a correct implementation of the Orders domain.

docs.title: Invariant List
docs.text: ID Invariant Enforced in INV-ORD-001 An Order must contain at least one line item Order.place() INV-ORD-002 All line item quantities must be positive integers Order.place() + OrderItem constructor INV-ORD-003 Status transitions must follow the defined state machine Order.transitionTo() guard INV-ORD-004 An Order cannot be confirmed without a payment reference Order.confirm() INV-ORD-005 An Order is immutable after reaching Confirmed status (except for the 30-minute cancellation window) Order.apply() guard INV-ORD-006 The order total must equal the sum of (unit price × quantity) for all line items Order.place() via OrderTotalCalculator INV-ORD-007 Stock must be reserved before payment is captured Saga orchestration order: ReserveStock → CapturePayment INV-ORD-008 A cancelled order's stock reservation must be released Saga compensation: ReleaseReservation before OrderCancelled is appended INV-ORD-009 The customer must not be in Suspended status at order placement Order.place() reads CustomerStatus from context INV-ORD-010 Order IDs must be globally unique EventStoreDB optimistic concurrency (expected version check)

docs.title: Valid Status Transitions (INV-ORD-003)
docs.text:
Pending → AwaitingPayment    (after StockReserved)
Pending → Cancelled          (customer request or saga failure)
AwaitingPayment → Confirmed  (after PaymentCaptured)
AwaitingPayment → Cancelled  (payment failure — compensation)
Confirmed → Cancelled        (customer request, within 30 minutes)

Any other transition is a domain error and must be rejected before any event is appended.

docs.title: Enforcement Strategy
docs.text:
Aggregate root guards: Each Order method checks pre-conditions before appending an event. If a pre-condition fails, a named DomainError is thrown (e.g., InvalidStatusTransitionError, EmptyOrderError).

EventStoreDB optimistic concurrency: The expected stream version is passed with every AppendToStream call. If another writer has appended since the aggregate was loaded, EventStoreDB rejects the write with a WrongExpectedVersionError — enforcing INV-ORD-010.

Saga orchestration layer: INV-ORD-007 and INV-ORD-008 are enforced at the application (saga) layer, not the domain layer, because they involve I/O. The saga will not proceed to CapturePayment until StockReserved is confirmed; it will not append OrderCancelled until ReservationReleased is confirmed.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Chakra Commerce is divided into four bounded contexts. Within each context, the model is consistent and the ubiquitous language applies without qualification. Across context boundaries, integration happens through versioned events and APIs — never through shared databases or direct object references.

docs.title: Context Map
docs.text:
graph TD
    subgraph orders_ctx ["Orders Context (core)"]
        ORD[Order Aggregate\nOrderPlaced · OrderConfirmed · OrderCancelled]
    end

    subgraph inventory_ctx ["Inventory Context (supporting)"]
        INV[StockItem Aggregate\nStockReserved · StockReleased]
    end

    subgraph customers_ctx ["Customers Context (supporting)"]
        CUS[Customer Aggregate\nCustomerRegistered · CustomerSuspended]
    end

    subgraph payment_ctx ["Payment Context (external)"]
        PAY[Payment Gateway\nExternal system — Stripe/Adyen]
    end

    subgraph fulfillment_ctx ["Fulfillment Context (gateway)"]
        FUL[Fulfillment Gateway\nLeave-and-Layer proxy → WMS]
        WMS[Warehouse Management System\nLegacy on-prem — untouched]
    end

    ORD -->|"PlaceOrder saga: ReserveStock command\n(Orders → Inventory)"| INV
    INV -->|"StockReserved event\n(Inventory → Orders)"| ORD
    ORD -->|"Capture payment\n(via ACL)"| PAY
    PAY -->|"PaymentCaptured / PaymentFailed\n(via ACL)"| ORD
    CUS -->|"CustomerRegistered event\n(Customers → Orders read cache)"| ORD
    CUS -->|"CustomerSuspended event\n(Customers → Orders)"| ORD
    ORD -->|"OrderConfirmed event\n(choreography — Orders does not know consumer)"| FUL
    FUL -->|"Dispatch request\n(SOAP/HTTP — translated from domain types)"| WMS

    style orders_ctx fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6
    style inventory_ctx fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#22c55e
    style customers_ctx fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#eab308
    style payment_ctx fill:#f3f4f6,stroke:#9ca3af
    style fulfillment_ctx fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#ec4899

docs.title: Context Relationships
docs.text: Relationship Type Description Orders → Inventory Partnership Both teams coordinate on saga protocol and event schema evolution Orders → Customers Downstream (conformist) Orders consumes Customer events; conforms to Customer's model Orders → Payment Anti-corruption layer Payment Gateway uses external vocabulary; ACL translates to Orders model Inventory → Orders Upstream (open host) Inventory publishes a stable event API; Orders is one of many consumers Orders → Fulfillment Choreography (published event) Orders publishes OrderConfirmed; Fulfillment Gateway consumes independently Fulfillment → WMS Leave-and-layer proxy Gateway translates domain types to legacy SOAP; WMS is untouched

docs.title: Contexts Summary
docs.text: Context Type Core concept Owner team Extracted? Orders Core domain Order lifecycle + saga orders-team Phase 4 (planned) Inventory Supporting Stock reservation + CQRS inventory-team Phase 3 (in progress) Customers Supporting Customer registration customers-team Phase 2 (done) Payment External Card processing vendor N/A Fulfillment Gateway (leave-and-layer) WMS proxy; async dispatch platform-team Deployed (permanent until WMS replaced)

docs.title: Integration Patterns Used
docs.text:
Published Language (Events): Domain events defined in contracts/event-schemas/ are the primary integration mechanism. Consumers read from Kafka topics and maintain local projections.

Anti-Corruption Layer (Payment): The Orders service wraps the Payment Gateway API with a PaymentGatewayAdapter that translates external types (ChargeResult, RefundResult) into Orders domain types (PaymentCaptured, PaymentFailed).

Open Host Service (Inventory API): The Inventory service exposes a stable REST API for stock reservation commands. Multiple consumers (Orders, future warehouse system) can use it without knowing Inventory internals.

Leave-and-Layer (Fulfillment Gateway): The legacy Warehouse Management System is left exactly as-is. The Fulfillment Gateway is a new service that layers a modern async interface on top — subscribing to OrderConfirmed events on Kafka and translating them into WMS SOAP calls. The WMS vendor contract, deployment, and protocol are untouched. See ADR-0011 and the contrast with Strangler Fig in ADR-0003.

docs.title: Bounded Context Summary
docs.text:
The Orders context owns the lifecycle of a customer's purchase from intent (placement) to outcome (confirmation or cancellation). It is the saga orchestrator for the place-order flow and is responsible for coordinating with Inventory and Payment while maintaining the invariants defined in contracts/domain-invariants/orders-invariants.md.

Owner team: orders-team Data store: EventStoreDB (event sourcing per ADR-0006) Integration: Publishes to chakra.orders.* Kafka topics; consumes from chakra.inventory.* and chakra.customers.*

docs.title: Aggregates
docs.text:
Aggregate Root Entities Value Objects Order Order OrderItem Money, OrderStatus, ShippingAddress

Only Order and OrderItem are persistence concerns. Money, OrderStatus, and ShippingAddress are value objects — they have no identity outside the Order that contains them.

docs.title: Invariants Enforced
docs.text:
All invariants from orders-invariants.md are enforced at the aggregate boundary. An aggregate method that would violate an invariant must throw a domain error before any event is appended.

Invariant Enforced in INV-ORD-001: At least one item Order.place() INV-ORD-002: Positive quantities Order.place() + OrderItem constructor INV-ORD-003: Valid status transitions Order.transitionTo() guard INV-ORD-004: Payment reference on confirmation Order.confirm() INV-ORD-005: Immutable after confirmation Order.apply() guard INV-ORD-006: Total = sum of line items Order.place() INV-ORD-010: Unique IDs EventStoreDB optimistic concurrency

docs.title: Commands and Events
docs.text: Command Pre-condition Event appended Post-condition PlaceOrder Customer not suspended; items non-empty; total correct OrderPlaced Status = Pending MarkAwaitingPayment Status = Pending OrderAwaitingPayment Status = AwaitingPayment ConfirmOrder Status = AwaitingPayment; paymentRef present OrderConfirmed Status = Confirmed CancelOrder Status ∈ {Pending, AwaitingPayment} or (Confirmed and < 30 min) OrderCancelled Status = Cancelled

docs.title: State Machine
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Pending : PlaceOrder command

    Pending --> AwaitingPayment : saga advances after StockReserved
    Pending --> Cancelled : CancelOrder (customer or system)

    AwaitingPayment --> Confirmed : ConfirmOrder (payment captured)
    AwaitingPayment --> Cancelled : CancelOrder (payment failed — compensation)

    Confirmed --> Cancelled : CancelOrder (< 30 min, customer-initiated)
    Confirmed --> [*] : fulfillment begins (out of Orders scope)

    Cancelled --> [*]

docs.title: Repository Contract
docs.text:
interface OrderRepository {
  save(order: Order): Promise<void>;
  findById(orderId: string): Promise<Order | null>;
  // Saves by appending uncommitted events to the EventStoreDB stream.
  // findById reconstructs the aggregate from the event stream.
  // Expected version is passed to enforce optimistic concurrency (INV-ORD-010).
}

docs.title: Domain Services
docs.text:
OrderTotalCalculator: Validates and calculates the order total from line items. Extracted as a domain service because the calculation logic may involve tax rules that are external to the Order aggregate. The aggregate delegates the calculation check to this service during Place.

PlaceOrderSagaOrchestrator (application layer, not domain): Coordinates the sequence: reserve stock → capture payment → confirm order. Lives in the application layer, not the domain layer, because it involves I/O and depends on external services.

docs.title: Data Migration Patterns
docs.text: Each bounded context extraction requires migrating data from the shared Oracle monolith database into a service-owned store. This document covers the three patterns used and their applicability.

docs.title: Pattern 1: CDC via Debezium (Historical Data)
docs.text:
Used to bootstrap a new service's data store with existing records from Oracle without downtime.

Oracle (monolith) → Debezium connector → Kafka topic → Consumer → Postgres (new service)

Process: 1. Deploy the Debezium Oracle connector pointed at the source table. 2. Debezium performs a full table snapshot — all existing rows become change events. 3. The bootstrap consumer writes each event into the new service's Postgres schema, applying any necessary transformations (column renames, type coercions, normalization). 4. Ongoing changes are captured as incremental CDC events until the dual-write phase begins.

Go-condition for Phase 3 (Inventory): CDC lag < 1 second for 30 consecutive minutes before traffic canary starts.

docs.title: Pattern 2: Dual-Write (Transition Window)
docs.text:
Used during the canary traffic ramp to guarantee no data is lost if a rollback is required.

Process: 1. New service receives writes (from the canary percentage of traffic). 2. A sidecar relay also writes the same mutation to Oracle via the monolith's internal API. 3. A reconciliation job (tooling/reconcile-dual-write.py) compares row counts and checksums between Oracle and the new store every 5 minutes. 4. If divergence > 0.1%, the reconciliation job pages on-call and halts canary advancement.

Invariant: Neither store is the "source of truth" during dual-write. Rollback is always possible because Oracle is still consistent.

docs.title: Pattern 3: Event Replay (Orders — Event Sourcing)
docs.text:
The Orders domain does not migrate rows — it migrates business events. Historical orders are replayed from Oracle's audit tables as synthetic domain events into EventStoreDB.

Process: 1. tooling/backfill-orders-eventstore.py reads the Oracle orders and order_audit tables. 2. For each order, it reconstructs the event sequence (OrderPlaced → OrderConfirmed | OrderCancelled) from the audit timestamps. 3. Events are appended to EventStoreDB streams. Optimistic concurrency (expected version = stream position) prevents duplicates on retry. 4. Projections are rebuilt from the backfilled streams before traffic is cut over.

Note: Financial reconciliation must validate that the projected order totals from EventStoreDB match Oracle to the cent before Phase 4 traffic canary begins.

docs.title: Rollback Procedures
docs.text:
For all phases, data migration rollback means: 1. Stop canary traffic (set Kong weight to 0 for the new service's paths). 2. Allow dual-write to drain (reconciliation job confirms Oracle is consistent). 3. Decommission the new service's Debezium connector and bootstrap consumer.

The monolith Oracle database is never modified during extraction — it is always the safe fallback state.

See also: Migration Strategy Overview

docs.title: Phase 1: Facade Layer
docs.text: Status: Done Risk: Low — Kong sits in front of the monolith; all traffic still goes to the monolith on miss. Rollback cost: Low — remove Kong from the load balancer; traffic returns directly to the monolith.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Establish the Kong API gateway as a routing facade without changing any monolith behavior. This is the prerequisite for all subsequent extractions — you cannot route traffic to new services until the routing layer exists.

docs.title: What Changes
docs.text: Before After Load balancer → Monolith (direct) Load balancer → Kong → Monolith (all traffic) No per-path routing Kong routes all paths to monolith by default No JWT validation at edge Kong validates JWT tokens; monolith receives pre-authenticated requests No distributed tracing at edge Kong propagates traceparent headers; OTEL pipeline receives Kong spans

docs.title: Go / No-Go Criteria
docs.text:
Go condition: All monolith paths return the same response with Kong in the path as without (verified by traffic shadow/comparison test for 24 hours at 100% weight).

No-go signals: - Latency p99 increase > 5ms (Kong overhead budget) - Any 5xx introduced by Kong routing - JWT validation false positives (valid tokens rejected)

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Update load balancer target group to point back to the monolith ALB directly.
  2. Kong stack remains deployed but receives no traffic — safe to decommission after 48-hour observation.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• ADR-0003: Strangler Fig Migration
  • Migration Strategy

docs.title: Phase 2: Extract Customers
docs.text: Status: Done Risk: Low — Customers has no upstream dependencies at the service boundary. Rollback cost: Low — re-route /v1/customers/* to monolith in Kong; no data loss if dual-write is healthy.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Customers is the lowest-risk extraction: smallest domain, simplest data model, no dependencies on Orders or Inventory at the service boundary. Extracting it first gives the team a full practice run of the extraction workflow (CDC bootstrap, dual-write, traffic canary, SLA validation) before tackling CQRS and event sourcing.

docs.title: What Changes
docs.text: Concern Before After Customer data store Oracle (shared) PostgreSQL (per-service) /v1/customers/* handler Monolith Java EE Customers Service (TypeScript/Fastify) Customer events None CustomerRegistered, AddressUpdated on Kafka SLA measurement None customers-sla.yaml → SLO → Prometheus alerts

docs.title: Extraction Sequence
docs.text:
1. Bootstrap data: Debezium CDC from Oracle customers table → chakra.customers.bootstrap topic → Postgres.
  2. Dual-write enabled: Customers Service writes to Postgres; relay also writes to Oracle.
  3. Canary start: Kong routes 1% of /v1/customers/* to Customers Service.
  4. Canary ramp: 1% → 10% → 50% → 100% (24-hour hold at each weight, SLA must hold).
  5. Dual-write disabled: Once at 100%, relay is stopped. Oracle customers table becomes stale.
  6. Monolith handler decommissioned: After 30 days at 100% with SLA compliance, the monolith customer handler is removed.

docs.title: Go / No-Go Criteria
docs.text:
• Customers Service p99 latency ≤ 300ms at all canary weights
  • 0 data divergence between Postgres and Oracle during dual-write (reconciliation job confirms)
  • CustomerRegistered events appearing in Kafka for all new registrations

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Kong: set /v1/customers/* weight to 0 for Customers Service (monolith receives 100%).
  2. If dual-write is active: no data loss. If dual-write is disabled: Oracle may be stale — restore from Postgres via reverse CDC if required.
  3. Trigger: SLA burn rate > 2× for 15 minutes, or data divergence > 0.1%.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Migration Strategy
  • Data Migration Patterns
  • ADR-0005: Database per Service

docs.title: Phase 3: Extract Inventory
docs.text: Status: In progress Risk: Medium — CQRS introduces a read/write split; CDC pipeline is the critical path. Rollback cost: Medium — Redis projection must be drained and reconciled; consumer lag reconciliation required before re-routing to monolith.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Extract the Inventory context with its CQRS architecture. The key challenge is the Oracle → Postgres CDC pipeline and the dual-write window, which involves both the PostgreSQL write model and the Redis read projection.

docs.title: What Changes
docs.text: Concern Before After Inventory data store Oracle (shared) PostgreSQL write model + Redis read cache Stock level reads Direct Oracle queries Redis GET (sub-1ms) Stock reservations Monolith Oracle transactions Inventory Service with row-level locking /v1/inventory/* handler Monolith Java EE Inventory Service (TypeScript/Fastify) Stock events None StockReserved, StockReleased on Kafka

docs.title: Extraction Sequence
docs.text:
1. Bootstrap write model: Debezium CDC from Oracle inventory table → Postgres.
  2. Bootstrap read model: Kafka consumer reads CDC stream and populates Redis projection.
  3. Verify CQRS parity: Reconciliation job confirms Redis stock levels match Postgres stock levels (tolerance: 0).
  4. Dual-write enabled: Inventory Service writes to Postgres; relay writes to Oracle.
  5. Canary start: Kong routes 1% of /v1/inventory/* to Inventory Service.
  6. Canary ramp: 1% → 10% → 50% → 100% (24-hour hold at each weight).
  7. Dual-write disabled: At 100%, relay stopped.
  8. 30-day stability window: Must be stable before Phase 4 (Orders) begins.

docs.title: Critical Path: CDC Pipeline
docs.text:
The CDC pipeline must drain (lag < 1 second for 30 minutes) before the traffic canary starts. If the pipeline falls behind during the canary, the reconciliation job halts canary advancement automatically.

Monitor: kafka_consumer_group_lag{group="inventory-bootstrap-consumer"} in Grafana.

docs.title: Go / No-Go Criteria
docs.text:
• CDC lag < 1 second for 30 minutes before canary starts
  • Stock level divergence = 0 (zero oversell tolerance — INV-INV-001)
  • Inventory Service read p99 ≤ 100ms (Redis projection, all canary weights)
  • StockReserved events appearing in Kafka for all order-driven reservations

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Kong: set /v1/inventory/* weight to 0 (monolith receives 100%).
  2. If dual-write active: Oracle is consistent. Verify Postgres matches Oracle before stopping relay.
  3. Redis projection: drain by stopping Kafka consumer; it is rebuilt from the Kafka topic on next start.
  4. Trigger: SLA burn rate > 2× for 15 minutes, Kafka lag > 10 seconds for 5 minutes, or oversell event detected.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Migration Strategy
  • ADR-0007: CQRS for Inventory
  • ADR-0005: Database per Service
  • Data Migration Patterns

docs.title: Phase 4: Extract Orders
docs.text: Status: Planned Prerequisite: Phase 3 (Inventory) stable for 30 days. Risk: High — event sourcing is a significant departure from the monolith's CRUD model; saga coordination with Inventory and Payment requires both services to be healthy. Rollback cost: High — EventStoreDB rollback requires replaying to a known checkpoint; saga compensation must be manually verified.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Extract the Orders domain using event sourcing (EventStoreDB) and saga orchestration. This is the most complex extraction because: 1. The data model changes fundamentally (CRUD rows → event streams). 2. The saga coordinates across two already-extracted services (Inventory) and an external system (Payment Gateway). 3. Historical orders must be backfilled as synthetic events, not rows.

docs.title: What Changes
docs.text: Concern Before After Orders data store Oracle (mutable rows) EventStoreDB (append-only event streams) Order lifecycle Monolith Java EE Orders Service (TypeScript/Fastify) Distributed transaction Implicit DB transaction Saga orchestration (reserve → pay → confirm) /v1/orders/* handler Monolith Java EE Orders Service Order events None OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, OrderCancelled on Kafka

docs.title: Extraction Sequence
docs.text:
1. Backfill EventStoreDB: tooling/backfill-orders-eventstore.py reconstructs historical order events from Oracle audit tables.
  2. Projection validation: Verify that reconstructed projections match Oracle order totals to the cent.
  3. Dual-write enabled: Orders Service appends events to EventStoreDB; relay writes to Oracle orders table.
  4. Canary start: Kong routes 1% of /v1/orders/* to Orders Service.
  5. Saga verification: Confirm that stock reservation and payment capture work end-to-end at canary weight.
  6. Canary ramp: 1% → 10% → 50% → 100%.
  7. Dual-write disabled: Oracle orders table becomes stale at 100%.
  8. 30-day stability window: Orders must be stable before Phase 5 (decommission) begins.

docs.title: Saga Validation at Each Canary Weight
docs.text: At each weight increase, run the saga integration test suite: - Happy path: place → reserve → pay → confirm - Compensation path: place → reserve → payment failure → release → cancel - Idempotency: duplicate PlaceOrder commands produce one OrderPlaced event (optimistic concurrency)

docs.title: Go / No-Go Criteria
docs.text:
• Financial reconciliation: EventStoreDB projections match Oracle to the cent
  • Saga happy path success rate ≥ 99.95% over 24 hours
  • Saga compensation triggers correctly on payment failure (verified by load test)
  • OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, OrderCancelled events appearing in Kafka
  • Fulfillment Gateway picking up OrderConfirmed and dispatching to WMS

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Kong: set /v1/orders/* weight to 0 (monolith receives 100%).
  2. If EventStoreDB has new events not in Oracle: run tooling/sync-eventstore-to-oracle.py to forward-sync.
  3. Trigger: Financial reconciliation divergence > 0, saga compensation failure, or SLA burn rate > 2× for 15 minutes.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Migration Strategy
  • ADR-0006: Event Sourcing for Orders
  • Sequence: Place Order
  • Sequence: Saga Compensation
  • Data Migration Patterns

docs.title: Phase 5: Decommission Monolith
docs.text: Status: Planned Prerequisite: Phase 4 (Orders) stable for 30 days; all traffic at 100% on new services. Risk: This phase is irreversible. The rollback path disappears when the monolith is shut down.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Shut down the legacy Java EE monolith and the shared Oracle database. At this point, all bounded contexts (Customers, Inventory, Orders) are independently deployed, SLA-compliant, and event-driven. The monolith is not needed as a fallback.

docs.title: Prerequisites Checklist
docs.text:
Before this phase begins, all of the following must be true:

  • Customers Service: 30 days at 100% canary weight, no SLA breaches
  • Inventory Service: 30 days at 100% canary weight, no SLA breaches
  • Orders Service: 30 days at 100% canary weight, no SLA breaches
  • Zero traffic to the monolith for 7 consecutive days (Kong access logs confirm)
  • Oracle database: no writes for 7 days (DBA confirms via AWR report)
  • Fulfillment Gateway: confirmed operational with OrderConfirmed events from Orders Service
  • All SLA files in contracts/slas/ reviewed and updated to reflect the new architecture
  • Engineering VP sign-off

docs.title: Decommission Sequence
docs.text:
1. Final snapshot: Take a full Oracle database export. Archive to S3 (7-year retention for financial audit).
  2. Remove Kong routes: Delete monolith route configs from Kong. All paths must now resolve to a new service or return 404.
  3. Drain monolith: Let the monolith process any in-flight requests (max 30 seconds). Scale to 0 replicas.
  4. Shutdown verification: Confirm 0 active connections to Oracle for 10 minutes.
  5. Terminate monolith ECS service: Remove from ECS cluster and Terraform state.
  6. Oracle decommission: Stop Oracle RDS instance. Take final snapshot. Schedule deletion (7-day retention before actual deletion).
  7. DNS cleanup: Remove any internal DNS records pointing to the monolith.
  8. Infrastructure cleanup: Remove Debezium connectors, dual-write relay, and reconciliation jobs.

docs.title: Post-Decommission Validation
docs.text:
• All /v1/* paths return valid responses from new services
  • Kafka topics contain no new messages from monolith producers
  • OTEL pipeline shows no spans from the monolith application
  • Cost report: Oracle RDS and ECS monolith cluster no longer appear in AWS Cost Explorer

docs.title: What Cannot Be Rolled Back
docs.text: Once the Oracle database is deleted (7 days after shutdown), the monolith cannot be restored. Ensure the S3 archive exists and is readable before proceeding.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Migration Strategy
  • ADR-0003: Strangler Fig Migration

docs.title: Goal
docs.text:
Migrate Chakra Commerce from a Java EE monolith to independent microservices with zero production downtime, each phase independently reversible, and measurable SLA compliance at each stage.

Success criteria: - Each extracted service meets its SLA for 30 days before the monolith handler is decommissioned - Zero order loss during any phase transition - Rollback to previous state possible within 10 minutes at any point

docs.title: Why Strangler Fig
docs.text:
A big-bang rewrite of a $2M/day revenue system is not a risk the business will accept. The strangler fig pattern allows us to:

  1. Deliver business value on the new architecture incrementally
  2. Validate each bounded context independently under real traffic
  3. Maintain a safe rollback path at every stage
  4. Avoid a "dual maintenance" period where two full systems must be kept in sync

docs.title: Phase Overview
docs.text: Phase What is extracted Key challenge Rollback cost Status 1 — Facade Kong gateway as routing facade Kong becomes critical path Low — remove Kong, traffic back to monolith Done 2 — Customers Customer registration + accounts Data sync from Oracle → Postgres Low — re-route /v1/customers/* to monolith Done 3 — Inventory Stock levels + reservations CDC pipeline; dual-write window Medium — consumer lag reconciliation In progress 4 — Orders Order lifecycle + saga Event sourcing adoption; saga coordination High — EventStoreDB rollback is complex Planned 5 — Decommission Monolith shutdown All traffic on new services; rollback path gone Irreversible Planned

docs.title: Extraction Order Rationale
docs.text:
Customers first: No upstream dependencies at the service boundary. The Customers context has the simplest domain model and the lowest blast radius if something goes wrong. Extracting it first gives the team practice with the full extraction workflow (CDC, dual-write, traffic routing, SLA validation) before tackling more complex contexts.

Inventory second: Moderate complexity. The CQRS read model introduces the first non-trivial architectural pattern. The CDC pipeline (Oracle → Kafka → Postgres) is the critical path for this phase. Inventory has no dependency on Customers at the service level (it consumes CustomerRegistered events only for cache warming, not for correctness).

Orders last: Highest complexity. Event sourcing is a significant departure from the monolith's CRUD model. The place-order saga coordinates with Inventory (which must be fully extracted) and Payment. This phase is not started until Inventory has been stable for 30 days.

docs.title: Data Migration Approach
docs.text:
See data-migration-patterns.md for details.

Dual-write: During extraction, the new service and the monolith both write to their respective stores. The monolith writes to Oracle; the new service writes to its Postgres. A reconciliation job compares both stores and alerts on divergence > 0.1%.

CDC via Debezium: For historical data (existing customer records, stock levels), Debezium captures change events from Oracle and replays them into the new service's Postgres schema. The CDC pipeline must drain before traffic is shifted.

Traffic canary: New service traffic starts at 1%. If SLA holds for 24 hours, ramp to 10%, then 50%, then 100%. If SLA degrades at any weight, roll back to the previous weight.

docs.title: Rollback Gates (per phase)
docs.text:
A phase may not advance without explicit approval. A phase is rolled back automatically if: - SLA breach (burn rate > 2x for 15 minutes) - Data divergence > 0.1% for > 5 minutes - Kafka consumer lag > 10 seconds for > 5 minutes (phases 3+)

Manual rollback procedure: docs/runbooks/rollback-deployment.md

docs.title: Phase Docs
docs.text:
• Phase 1: Facade Layer
  • Phase 2: Extract Customers
  • Phase 3: Extract Inventory
  • Phase 4: Extract Orders
  • Phase 5: Decommission Monolith

docs.title: Pattern Summary
docs.text:
The Chakra Commerce Warehouse Management System (WMS) is an on-premises vendor product that cannot be modified. Rather than building a modern replacement in parallel with the Orders extraction, we apply the Leave-and-Layer pattern:

  • Leave: The WMS operates exactly as it did under the monolith. Its SOAP API, deployment, and vendor contract are untouched.
  • Layer: A new Fulfillment Gateway service sits between the Kafka event bus and the WMS. It subscribes to OrderConfirmed events and translates them into WMS fulfillment requests.

This is not a migration toward a new warehouse system. It is an interface modernization. The WMS remains the system of record for physical fulfillment until a future initiative replaces it.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Orders service
    │
    │  publishes OrderConfirmed
    ▼
Kafka topic: chakra.orders.confirmed
    │
    │  consumer group: fulfillment-gateway
    ▼
Fulfillment Gateway (new service)
    │
    │  translates domain event → WMS dispatch request
    │  SOAP/HTTP with circuit breaker + retry
    ▼
Warehouse Management System (untouched legacy)

The Orders service does not know the WMS exists. It publishes a domain event and its responsibility ends. The Fulfillment Gateway owns the WMS integration concern entirely.

docs.title: Phase A — Gateway deployed alongside monolith
docs.text:
1. Deploy Fulfillment Gateway to chakra-fulfillment namespace.
  2. Gateway consumer group fulfillment-gateway starts from the latest Kafka offset.
  3. The monolith continues to call the WMS directly via its existing integration path.
  4. Both paths active in parallel — this is intentional. Duplicate fulfillment requests to WMS are idempotent (WMS deduplicates on order ID).
  5. Validate: Kafka consumer lag stays below 30 seconds; WMS receives gateway-originated calls; circuit breaker remains closed.

docs.title: Phase B — Monolith fulfillment path disabled
docs.text:
Prerequisite: Gateway has been stable (P99 < 2000ms, availability > 99.9%) for 14 days.

  1. Disable the monolith's WMS integration in Kong routing config (one-line change).
  2. All fulfillment flow now runs through the Fulfillment Gateway only.
  3. Validate: WMS call volume matches order confirmation rate; no fulfillment lag alerts firing.

docs.title: Phase C — Orders extraction complete (Phase 4)
docs.text: When the Orders service is extracted from the monolith: - No changes to the Fulfillment Gateway are required. - The gateway consumes from the same Kafka topic. The producer changes from monolith to Orders service; the consumer sees no difference.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text: Phase Rollback action Time to effect Phase A Stop Fulfillment Gateway; monolith path already active < 1 minute Phase B Re-enable monolith WMS integration in Kong config < 2 minutes Phase C No rollback needed; gateway is unchanged by Orders extraction

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Gateway behavior WMS impact WMS SOAP timeout Retry with exponential backoff (3 attempts, max 30s) WMS receives up to 3 attempts; deduplicates on order ID WMS returns 5xx Circuit breaker opens after 5 consecutive failures; events accumulate in Kafka No WMS calls during open window (30s); consumer lag alert fires Fulfillment Gateway pod crash Kafka offset not committed; events replayed on restart Duplicate call possible; WMS deduplicates Kafka broker unavailable Gateway cannot consume; no WMS calls No fulfillment lag; alert fires at 30s consumer lag threshold

docs.title: SLA Relationship
docs.text:
The Fulfillment Gateway has its own SLA (contracts/slas/fulfillment-sla.yaml) that is independent of the Orders SLA. A WMS outage that causes the gateway circuit breaker to open: - Does not breach the Orders SLA — order placement and confirmation continue normally. - Does breach the Fulfillment SLA — fulfillment dispatch success rate drops.

This separation is intentional. The leave-and-layer boundary isolates WMS unreliability from the core commerce platform SLAs.

docs.title: Exit Criteria
docs.text:
The Fulfillment Gateway is decommissioned when:

  1. A modern Fulfillment service (with its own persistence, domain model, and SLA) is validated against the WMS functional contract.
  2. The modern service's consumer group takes over the chakra.orders.confirmed topic.
  3. The WMS vendor contract is allowed to lapse or the WMS is decommissioned.

No changes are required in Orders, Inventory, or Customers when the gateway exits.

docs.title: Observability
docs.text: SLA measurement is not an afterthought in this architecture. It is woven in from the first contract file to the last Prometheus alert. Every numeric target is written once in contracts/slas/ and flows downstream through a deterministic pipeline to runtime alerts.

docs.title: The SLA → Alert Pipeline
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A["contracts/slas/*.yaml\n(human-authored)"]
    B["tooling/generate-prometheus-rules.py\n(deterministic script)"]
    C["observability/slos/*.yaml\n(30-day windows, error budgets)"]
    D["observability/alerts/*-burnrate.yaml\n(PrometheusRule)"]
    E["Grafana / PagerDuty"]

    A -->|"script reads"| B
    B -->|"writes"| C
    B -->|"writes"| D
    D -->|"alerts fire into"| E
    C -->|"queried by"| E

A target is written once — in contracts/slas/. The script is idempotent. Running it again produces identical output. validate-contracts.sh in CI verifies every SLA file has a matching SLO file and a matching alert file.

See the SLA Measurement Flow diagram for the full signal path from service instrumentation to on-call alert.

docs.title: Multi-Window Burn Rate Alerting
docs.text:
Following the Google SRE model, each SLO has two alert tiers:

Tier Window Burn rate threshold Action Alert for Fast burn 1 hour 14.4× Page on-call immediately Consuming 1% of monthly budget per hour Slow burn 6 hours 6× Create ticket Sustained degradation that won't page but will exhaust budget

The 14.4× rate means: if continued for the whole window, 100% of the monthly error budget would be consumed. The 6× rate means the same across 6 hours. Both tiers fire for: 2m and for: 15m respectively, suppressing transient spikes.

Reference Implementation

File What it contains observability/alerts/orders-burnrate.yaml Fast + slow burn for Orders availability and latency; saga compensation rate alert observability/alerts/inventory-burnrate.yaml Reservation success rate; read latency; consumer lag observability/alerts/customers-burnrate.yaml Registration latency; GDPR deletion SLA observability/alerts/platform-burnrate.yaml EKS availability; node health

docs.title: Histogram Buckets Derived from SLA Targets
docs.text:
The OTEL histogram buckets in each service are not arbitrary. They are generated to include the SLA latency_p99_ms target as a bucket boundary.

For Orders (latency_p99_ms: 500), the bucket at 0.5s must exist so that histogram_quantile(0.99, ...) can resolve exactly at the SLA threshold:

// services/orders/src/infrastructure/OtelInstrumentation.ts
const LATENCY_BUCKETS = [0.005, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.5];
//                                                              ^^^
//                                              SLA p99 = 500ms → 0.5s boundary

If the bucket does not exist, histogram_quantile(0.99, ...) interpolates between 0.25 and 1.0 — the result is inaccurate relative to the SLA. The compliance agent checks this in Phase 5.

Reference Implementation

File Bucket boundary services/orders/src/infrastructure/OtelInstrumentation.ts 0.5s (Orders p99 = 500ms) services/fulfillment-gateway/src/infrastructure/OtelInstrumentation.ts 2.0s (Fulfillment WMS p99 = 2000ms)

docs.title: Three Pillars via One Pipeline
docs.text:
All telemetry flows through a single OTEL pipeline. Services emit to localhost:4317 (the OTEL Collector sidecar — see Sidecar pattern). The collector forwards to:

Signal Backend Used for Traces Grafana Tempo Distributed tracing; full saga chain reconstruction Metrics Grafana Mimir SLO queries, burn rate alerts, dashboards Logs Grafana Loki Structured log search; correlated with traces via traceId

Reference Implementation

File Purpose observability/otel/collector-config.yaml OTLP receive → batch processor → Tempo/Mimir/Loki exporters observability/otel/instrumentation-cr.yaml OTEL Operator Instrumentation CR: auto-injects collector into all chakra-* namespaces ai-agents/context/observability-requirements.md Required metric names, span names, log fields — the standard every agent-built service must meet

docs.title: SLO Files
docs.text:
Each SLO YAML file repeats the SLA target into a Prometheus-queryable specification with window, error budget minutes, and alert tier thresholds:

SLO file Availability target Error budget p99 latency orders-slo.yaml 99.95% 21.6 min/month 500ms inventory-slo.yaml 99.9% 43.2 min/month read 100ms customers-slo.yaml 99.9% 43.2 min/month 300ms platform-slo.yaml 99.95% 21.6 min/month —

docs.title: Runbooks
docs.text: When a burn rate alert fires, the SLA Breach Response runbook provides a structured response: fast burn vs. slow burn triage, 5-step process, and escalation matrix.

docs.title: Pattern Catalogue
docs.text:
Ten architecture patterns are demonstrated in this reference implementation. Each is backed by an Architecture Decision Record, a concrete implementation in the codebase, and cross-references to the SLA contracts that motivate it.

Patterns are grouped by concern. Many interact — the pattern interaction map at the bottom shows how.

docs.title: Strangler Fig
docs.text:
ADR: ADR-0003 · Type: Migration

The Kong API gateway is deployed as a facade in front of the Java EE monolith from day one. Traffic is routed by URL path prefix. As each bounded context is extracted, its paths are re-routed from the monolith to the new service. The monolith shrinks one context at a time; it is never rewritten.

Each phase ends with a validation period (SLA compliance confirmed) before the monolith handlers for that context are disabled. Any phase can be reversed within 5 minutes by updating Kong routing config.

Where it lives in the codebase

Artifact Purpose docs/migration/strategy.md 5-phase migration plan with rollback gates infrastructure/helm/charts/api-gateway/ Kong Helm chart with strangler-fig routing config docs/adrs/ADR-0003 Why not a big-bang rewrite

Key constraint: Each extraction is independently reversible. The monolith is not modified — only Kong routing changes.

docs.title: Leave and Layer
docs.text:
ADR: ADR-0011 · Type: Migration / Integration

The Warehouse Management System (WMS) is a vendor on-prem product that cannot be modified. Rather than replacing it in parallel with the Orders extraction, the Fulfillment Gateway is deployed as a new asynchronous layer: it subscribes to OrderConfirmed events on Kafka and translates them to WMS SOAP calls.

This is the critical distinction from Strangler Fig: Strangler Fig replaces the old system. Leave-and-Layer accepts it as a long-lived dependency and adapts to it. The WMS vendor contract, SOAP API, and deployment are completely untouched.

Where it lives in the codebase

Artifact Purpose services/fulfillment-gateway/src/infrastructure/WarehouseClient.ts The layer: translates domain types to legacy SOAP vocabulary services/fulfillment-gateway/src/application/OrderConfirmedHandler.ts Kafka consumer — the entry point contracts/slas/fulfillment-sla.yaml Independent SLA for the gateway; WMS outage ≠ Orders SLA breach docs/migration/warehouse-leave-and-layer.md Deployment phases and rollback

Key insight: WarehouseClient.ts contains two vocabulary layers. WmsDispatchRequest (private type, matches 2004 SOAP schema) is the legacy surface. FulfillmentRequest (exported) is the domain surface. Nothing outside WarehouseClient.ts knows WmsDispatchRequest exists.

docs.title: Event Sourcing
docs.text:
ADR: ADR-0006 · Type: Data / State Management

The Orders domain uses EventStoreDB as its system of record. Every state transition appends an immutable event to the order's stream. Current state is reconstructed by replaying the stream. The mutable row is gone.

Three properties justify event sourcing specifically for Orders (not for Inventory or Customers):

  1. Full audit trail — every price change, cancellation reason, and address update is in the stream permanently
  2. Saga compensation — when the place-order saga must be rolled back, the event stream gives a causal chain to replay in a test context before committing compensation
  3. Temporal queries — "what was the state of this order at 14:32?" is a stream replay, not a dedicated audit table

Where it lives in the codebase

Artifact Purpose services/orders/src/domain/Order.ts Aggregate root: reconstitute() replays events, apply() dispatches each services/orders/src/domain/OrderStatus.ts State machine with assertValidTransition() guard services/orders/src/infrastructure/OrderRepository.ts EventStoreDB-backed repository stub contracts/event-schemas/ Canonical JSON Schemas for all domain events

Key constraint: Event sourcing is used only in the Orders domain (ADR-0006). Inventory and Customers use PostgreSQL CRUD with row versioning.

docs.title: CQRS
docs.text:
ADR: ADR-0007 · Type: Data / Performance

Inventory has a 10:1 read-to-write ratio. Stock level queries must be sub-10ms; reservation writes need strong consistency. A single PostgreSQL store cannot satisfy both requirements at the required throughput.

The write model (PostgreSQL) handles all ReserveStock and ReleaseStock commands with row-level locking. A Kafka consumer maintains a Redis hash projection of current stock levels. Reads go to Redis exclusively.

The two models diverge by at most one Kafka consumer lag interval (target: <100ms). This divergence is explicitly documented in the Inventory SLA and enforced by a consumer lag alert.

Where it lives in the codebase

Artifact Purpose contracts/domain-invariants/inventory-invariants.md INV-INV-006: Redis is not authoritative for write decisions contracts/slas/inventory-sla.yaml Read p99 100ms; write p99 300ms; oversell tolerance 0 observability/slos/inventory-slo.yaml SLO spec derived from the SLA observability/alerts/inventory-burnrate.yaml Burn rate alerts for reservation success rate and read latency

Key constraint: INV-INV-006 states that no write decision may use the Redis projection as input. A reservation that reads from Redis to check stock could race with an uncommitted write. Aggregates read from PostgreSQL; Redis serves only query endpoints.

docs.title: Outbox Pattern
docs.text:
Referenced in: ADR-0006, ADR-0004 · Type: Data / Reliability

Domain events are never published directly from command handlers. A dedicated outbox process reads the EventStoreDB stream and publishes to Kafka topics. This decouples the command commit from the Kafka publish and guarantees at-least-once delivery without distributed transactions.

Where it lives in the codebase

Artifact Purpose services/orders/src/infrastructure/KafkaEventPublisher.ts Outbox consumer stub: reads EventStoreDB, publishes to Kafka ai-agents/tasks/agent/architectural-compliance-review.md Phase 5 check: events published via outbox, not directly from command handlers

docs.title: Saga Orchestration
docs.text:
ADR: ADR-0006 · Type: Integration / Distributed Transactions

The place-order flow is a distributed transaction across three systems. The Orders service acts as the orchestrator: it issues commands, waits for outcomes, and triggers compensation when a step fails.

PlaceOrder command
  ↓
1. ReserveStock (Inventory) — wait for StockReserved
  ↓
2. CapturePayment (Payment Gateway) — wait for PaymentCaptured
  ↓ (if PaymentFailed)
  → ReleaseStock (compensation) → OrderCancelled
  ↓ (if PaymentCaptured)
3. ConfirmOrder → OrderConfirmed published

Where it lives in the codebase

Artifact Purpose services/orders/src/application/PlaceOrderCommand.ts Saga orchestrator skeleton services/orders/src/domain/Order.ts Enforces INV-ORD-007: stock reserved before payment captured contracts/slas/orders-sla.yaml saga_compensation.max_ms: 5000 — compensation must complete within 5s observability/alerts/orders-burnrate.yaml OrdersSagaCompensationRateHigh alert fires when compensation rate exceeds 5%

Key constraint: Compensation events must be published via the outbox (not in-memory) so they survive a crash during the compensation phase. The saga_compensation.max_ms SLA target is enforced by a histogram bucket in OtelInstrumentation.ts.

docs.title: Choreography
docs.text:
ADR: ADR-0015 · Type: Integration / Event-Driven

Orchestration is used when outcome matters. Choreography is used when it doesn't. When an order is confirmed, the Orders service publishes OrderConfirmed and is done — it has no knowledge of the Fulfillment Gateway, inventory cache consumers, or any future analytics pipeline.

Event Producer Consumers Why choreography OrderConfirmed Orders Fulfillment Gateway WMS failure ≠ Orders SLA breach CustomerRegistered Customers Inventory (cache warm) Cache miss is a performance issue, not a correctness issue CustomerSuspended Customers Orders (cache invalidation) Invalidation is eventually consistent by design

Where it lives in the codebase

Artifact Purpose docs/architecture/diagrams/sequence-choreography.md Sequence diagram: OrderConfirmed fan-out and independent consumer failure services/fulfillment-gateway/src/application/OrderConfirmedHandler.ts Primary choreography consumer api/asyncapi/orders-events-v1.yaml AsyncAPI contract listing all OrderConfirmed consumers

docs.title: Circuit Breaker
docs.text:
ADR: ADR-0012 · Type: Resilience

Two external calls in the system can fail unpredictably: Orders → Payment Gateway, and Fulfillment Gateway → WMS. Without a circuit breaker, a degraded downstream holds connections open until the caller's pool is exhausted.

The pattern is implemented at two levels:

Infrastructure level (Istio)Application level (WarehouseClient)

DestinationRule with outlierDetection in each service's Helm chart. No application code changes. Applied to all in-mesh calls.

outlierDetection:
  consecutive5xxErrors: 5
  interval: 30s
  baseEjectionTime: 30s
  maxEjectionPercent: 100

The WMS endpoint is out-of-mesh (on-prem SOAP). WarehouseClient.ts implements a three-state machine (closed/open/half-open) with thresholds sourced from contracts/slas/fulfillment-sla.yaml.

private enforceCircuitBreaker(): void {
  if (this.circuitState === 'open') {
    const elapsed = Date.now() - this.lastOpenedAt;
    if (elapsed >= this.OPEN_DURATION_MS) {
      this.circuitState = 'half-open'; // probe
    } else {
      throw new CircuitOpenError('WMS circuit breaker is open');
    }
  }
}

Where it lives in the codebase

Artifact Purpose services/fulfillment-gateway/src/infrastructure/WarehouseClient.ts Application-level state machine infrastructure/helm/charts/orders-service/templates/destination-rule.yaml Istio rule: Payment Gateway egress (tight: 3 failures, 60s ejection) infrastructure/helm/charts/fulfillment-gateway/templates/destination-rule.yaml Istio rule: in-mesh dependencies contracts/slas/fulfillment-sla.yaml Circuit breaker thresholds (human-authored source of truth)

Key constraint: The circuit_breaker_state gauge (0=closed, 1=open, 2=half-open) is a required OTEL metric for any service with external calls. The state must be observable in Grafana without log parsing.

docs.title: Bulkhead Isolation
docs.text:
ADR: ADR-0013 · Type: Resilience

Failures in the Fulfillment Gateway (a background service) must not be able to evict Orders pods or exhaust Orders' connection pools. Two isolation layers enforce this.

Namespace quotas (coarse)Connection pool limits (fine)

Each bounded context has a ResourceQuota that caps total CPU and memory. A Fulfillment Gateway memory leak hits the chakra-fulfillment quota before it can affect chakra-orders.

Namespace CPU limit Memory limit chakra-orders 6 cores 6Gi chakra-inventory 12 cores 12Gi chakra-customers 1.5 cores 1.5Gi chakra-fulfillment 1.2 cores 1.2Gi

Each service's DestinationRule sets explicit connectionPool limits per downstream. A degraded Payment Gateway can hold at most 20 TCP connections from the Orders service — additional requests get a fast 503, which the circuit breaker counts.

Where it lives in the codebase

Artifact Purpose infrastructure/kubernetes/policies/resource-quotas.yaml All five namespace quotas + LimitRanges with formula comments infrastructure/helm/charts/orders-service/templates/destination-rule.yaml connectionPool.tcp.maxConnections: 20 for Payment Gateway tooling/service-manifest.yaml Source of truth for resource sizing; quotas derived from limits × max_replicas × 1.2

docs.title: Sidecar
docs.text:
ADR: ADR-0014 · Type: Resilience / Observability

Three cross-cutting behaviors — mTLS, traffic policy, and telemetry — are moved out of application code into two injected sidecars. Service authors write business logic and OTEL SDK calls. They implement no TLS code, no retry logic, and no metrics transport.

Sidecar Injected by Responsibilities Envoy Istio control plane mTLS (SPIFFE/X.509), DestinationRule enforcement, trace context propagation OTEL Collector OpenTelemetry Operator Receives OTLP on localhost:4317, batches, forwards to Grafana Tempo/Mimir/Loki

Where it lives in the codebase

Artifact Purpose infrastructure/helm/charts/orders-service/templates/deployment.yaml Both injection annotations on the pod spec observability/otel/instrumentation-cr.yaml OTEL Operator Instrumentation CR: which namespaces get the collector services/orders/src/infrastructure/OtelInstrumentation.ts Application-side: creates meters, registers counters and histograms — no exporter config

Key constraint: The compliance agent (Phase 4 check) verifies sidecar.istio.io/inject: "true" is present in every Deployment and that PeerAuthentication mode is STRICT in every chakra-* namespace.

docs.title: Pattern Interaction Map
docs.text:
The patterns don't stand alone. Each one enables or constrains another.

graph TD
    SF[Strangler Fig\nKong facade] -->|extracts| ES[Event Sourcing\nOrders]
    SF -->|extracts| CQRS[CQRS\nInventory]
    SF -->|extracts| LL[Leave and Layer\nFulfillment Gateway]

    ES -->|requires| OB[Outbox Pattern\nKafkaEventPublisher]

    SAGA -->|compensation requires| ES

    LL -->|protected by| CB[Circuit Breaker\nWarehouseClient]

docs.title: Pattern Summary Table
docs.text: Pattern ADR Type Used in Strangler Fig ADR-0003 Migration All service extractions via Kong Leave and Layer ADR-0011 Migration Fulfillment Gateway → WMS Event Sourcing ADR-0006 Data Orders domain only CQRS ADR-0007 Data Inventory (PostgreSQL write + Redis read) Outbox ADR-0006 Data EventStoreDB → Kafka Saga Orchestration ADR-0006 Integration Place-order flow Choreography ADR-0015 Integration OrderConfirmed → Fulfillment, cache warm Circuit Breaker ADR-0012 Resilience Istio (in-mesh) + WarehouseClient (out-of-mesh) Bulkhead ADR-0013 Resilience Namespace quotas + connection pool limits Sidecar ADR-0014 Resilience / Observability Envoy + OTEL Collector in every pod

docs.title: Runbook: Database Failover
docs.text:
When to use: AWS RDS has triggered a failover (planned or unplanned) for one of the Chakra Commerce PostgreSQL instances. Services may be returning 5xx errors during the failover window (typically 30–60 seconds for RDS Multi-AZ).

Databases covered: | Database | RDS Identifier | Service | Namespace | |---|---|---|---| | Orders Event Store | Not RDS — EventStoreDB on EKS | Orders | chakra-orders | | Inventory PostgreSQL | chakra-inventory-postgres | Inventory | chakra-inventory | | Customers PostgreSQL | chakra-customers-postgres | Customers | chakra-customers |

docs.title: Step 1: Confirm Failover is in Progress
docs.text:
# Check RDS events for the affected instance
aws rds describe-events \
  --source-identifier chakra-{service}-postgres \
  --source-type db-instance \
  --duration 60 \
  | jq '.Events[] | {Message, Date}'

# Common event messages indicating failover:
# "Multi-AZ instance failover started"
# "DB instance restarted"
# "Multi-AZ instance failover completed"

A Multi-AZ failover is typically resolved within 60 seconds. If the event shows "failover completed" and services are still unhealthy, proceed to Step 2.

docs.title: Step 2: Verify DNS Resolution After Failover
docs.text:
RDS Multi-AZ uses a CNAME that automatically updates to the new primary after failover. Services use the CNAME endpoint — not a direct IP. However, the application connection pool may be holding stale connections.

# Check the CNAME resolves to the new primary
nslookup chakra-{service}-postgres.cluster-xxxxx.{region}.rds.amazonaws.com

# Force connection pool refresh by rolling the pods
kubectl rollout restart deploy/{service} -n chakra-{service}
kubectl rollout status deploy/{service} -n chakra-{service}

docs.title: Step 3: Verify Service Health After Pod Restart
docs.text:
# Check pods are running
kubectl get pods -n chakra-{service}

# Check database connectivity from the pod
kubectl exec -it deploy/{service} -n chakra-{service} -- \
  node -e "require('./dist/health').checkDb().then(console.log)"

# Or use the health endpoint
curl -s https://internal.chakracommerce.internal/v1/{service}/health | jq .

docs.title: Step 4: Check for Data Loss (EventStoreDB)
docs.text:
EventStoreDB (Orders) is not RDS and does not use Multi-AZ in the same way. It uses a 3-node cluster with leader election.

# Check EventStoreDB cluster state
kubectl exec -it eventstore-0 -n chakra-orders -- \
  curl -s http://localhost:2113/gossip | jq '.members[] | {state, instanceId}'

# Expected: one member with state "Leader", others "Follower"
# If no leader for > 30 seconds: escalate to platform team

docs.title: Step 5: Check for Outstanding Saga Compensation
docs.text:
If the Orders service lost connectivity to EventStoreDB or PostgreSQL during a saga (place-order flow), check for orphaned sagas:

# Find orders in AwaitingPayment state older than 5 minutes
# (These may have had payment captured but confirmation not yet appended)
kubectl exec -it deploy/orders -n chakra-orders -- \
  node -e "require('./dist/tools/find-orphaned-sagas').run()"

Orphaned sagas require manual review by the orders-team lead.

docs.title: Step 6: SLA Impact Assessment
docs.text:
Database failovers are the most common cause of latency spikes that exceed SLA targets. After recovery:

  1. Check the error budget remaining for the affected service in Grafana.
  2. If budget is < 20%: notify engineering VP and consider a feature freeze.
  3. Add a note to the post-incident review about the failover cause (AWS infrastructure event vs. operator error vs. known instance issue).

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
• All services use RDS Multi-AZ (both primary and standby in separate AZs).
  • Connection pools use keepAlive: true with a maximum connection age to avoid stale connections surviving a failover.
  • RDS Proxy is a future consideration — it maintains a warm connection pool and reduces failover client impact.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• SLA Breach Response
  • Rollback Deployment
  • ADR-0005: Database per Service

docs.title: Runbook: Kafka Consumer Lag
docs.text:
When to use: The KafkaConsumerLagHigh alert fires, or the SLA breach runbook identifies consumer lag as a contributing cause (e.g., Redis projection falling behind, Fulfillment Gateway delayed dispatches).

Alert thresholds: - Warning: consumer lag > 1,000 messages for > 2 minutes - Critical: consumer lag > 10,000 messages for > 5 minutes

docs.title: Step 1: Identify the Affected Consumer Group
docs.text:
# List all consumer groups and their lag
kafka-consumer-groups.sh \
  --bootstrap-server $KAFKA_BOOTSTRAP \
  --describe --all-groups \
  | grep -E "GROUP|LAG" \
  | sort -k6 -rn

# Or check the Grafana Kafka Overview dashboard
# Panel: "Consumer Group Lag by Group and Topic"

Common consumer groups: | Consumer Group | Service | Topic | |---|---|---| | inventory-reservation-consumer | Inventory | chakra.orders.placed | | inventory-redis-projection | Inventory (Redis) | chakra.inventory.reserved, chakra.inventory.released | | fulfillment-gateway-consumer | Fulfillment Gateway | chakra.orders.confirmed |

docs.title: Consumer is running but slow
docs.text:
# Check consumer pod CPU/memory
kubectl top pods -n chakra-{service}

# If CPU-throttled → increase resource limits or scale out
kubectl scale deploy/{service}-consumer -n chakra-{service} --replicas=3

docs.title: Consumer is not running (pod down)
docs.text:
kubectl get pods -n chakra-{service} | grep consumer
# If CrashLoopBackOff → check logs
kubectl logs -n chakra-{service} deploy/{service}-consumer --previous

docs.title: Topic partition count too low
docs.text:
# Check partition count vs consumer instances
kafka-topics.sh --bootstrap-server $KAFKA_BOOTSTRAP \
  --describe --topic chakra.{domain}.{event}
# If partitions < consumer instances → consumers sit idle
# Increasing partitions requires coordinated topic admin — escalate to platform team

docs.title: Step 3: Remediate
docs.text: Root cause Remediation Consumer pod down Fix crash cause; let pod restart; lag will drain Consumer CPU-throttled Scale horizontally; update HPA min replicas Temporary traffic spike Monitor drain rate; if draining, no action needed Partition count too low Escalate to platform team for partition increase (rolling) Poison pill message See below

docs.title: Handling a Poison Pill
docs.text:
If the consumer is crashing on a specific message: 

# Find the offset of the bad message
kubectl logs -n chakra-{service} deploy/{service}-consumer | grep "deserialize\|parse error"

# Skip the offset (use with caution — only for non-critical consumers)
kafka-consumer-groups.sh \
  --bootstrap-server $KAFKA_BOOTSTRAP \
  --group {consumer-group} \
  --topic chakra.{domain}.{event} \
  --reset-offsets --to-offset {bad_offset+1} \
  --execute

Do not skip offsets for inventory-reservation-consumer or fulfillment-gateway-consumer without engineering lead approval — these affect business correctness.

docs.title: Step 4: Verify Recovery
docs.text:
• Lag decreasing in Grafana Kafka Overview dashboard
  • Alert resolves within 15 minutes of remediation
  • inventory_projection_lag_seconds metric normalizing (for Redis projection consumers)

docs.title: Impact by Consumer Group
docs.text: Consumer Group Impact if lagged inventory-reservation-consumer New orders are not triggering stock reservations — saga stalls at OrderPlaced inventory-redis-projection Read model is stale — stock levels shown to customers are behind, but write consistency is maintained fulfillment-gateway-consumer Confirmed orders are not being dispatched to WMS — fulfillment delay, no data loss

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• SLA Breach Response
  • ADR-0004: Kafka Event Bus
  • ADR-0007: CQRS for Inventory

docs.title: Runbook: Rollback Deployment
docs.text: When to use: A recent deployment caused an SLA breach, crash loops, or a regression in business metrics. This runbook covers ArgoCD-managed Helm deployments (all chakra-* services).

docs.title: Step 1: Confirm the Deployment is the Cause
docs.text:
# Check when the last sync happened
argocd app history chakra-{service}

# Confirm the SLA alert timestamp aligns with the deployment window
# If the alert fired within 5 minutes of sync → deployment is suspected cause

If the alert preceded the deployment, the deployment is not the root cause — go back to SLA Breach Response.

docs.title: Step 2: Roll Back via ArgoCD
docs.text:
# List recent revisions
argocd app history chakra-{service}

# Roll back to the previous successful revision
argocd app rollback chakra-{service} <PREVIOUS_REVISION_ID>

# Watch the rollout
kubectl rollout status deploy/{service} -n chakra-{service}

ArgoCD rollback syncs the cluster state to the previous Helm chart revision. It does not modify Git — the repo still contains the bad version.

docs.title: Step 3: Verify Recovery
docs.text:
# Check pod health
kubectl get pods -n chakra-{service}

# Confirm no crash loops
kubectl describe deploy/{service} -n chakra-{service} | grep -A5 Conditions

# Check Grafana: SLA burn rate alert must resolve within 15 minutes

If pods are healthy but the burn rate alert is still firing after 15 minutes, the rollback may not have been the fix — see SLA Breach Response for other root cause categories.

docs.title: Step 4: Prevent Re-Deployment
docs.text:
ArgoCD will try to re-sync to the current Git HEAD (the bad version). To prevent this until the fix is merged:

# Suspend auto-sync for the application
argocd app set chakra-{service} --sync-policy none

Re-enable auto-sync once the fix is merged and validated in staging:

argocd app set chakra-{service} --sync-policy automated --self-heal

docs.title: Step 5: Post-Rollback Actions
docs.text:
1. Create an incident ticket with: deployment SHA, alert timestamp, rollback timestamp, impact duration.
  2. Fix the bad change in a new PR. Do not push to main until the fix is tested.
  3. Re-enable ArgoCD auto-sync after the fix is merged.
  4. Update this runbook if a new rollback scenario was discovered.

docs.title: Rollback During Migration Canary
docs.text:
If the rollback is for a canary-weight service (during active migration), also:

  1. Set the Kong canary weight to 0% for the affected service path.
  2. Confirm dual-write relay is still running (if applicable).
  3. Notify the migration team — the canary ramp is paused until the fix is validated.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• SLA Breach Response
  • Migration Strategy
  • ADR-0009: Helm GitOps

docs.title: Runbook: SLA Breach Response
docs.text: Severity: P1 (fast burn) / P2 (slow burn) Alert: *FastBurn (severity: page) / *SlowBurn (severity: ticket)

docs.title: What Fired
docs.text:
A burn rate alert means error budget is being consumed faster than the allocation rate. The alert name tells you: - Which service (Orders, Inventory, Customers) - Which SLO dimension (Availability or Latency) - Which tier (FastBurn = pages now / SlowBurn = fix today)

Alert tier Burn rate Budget exhaustion Action FastBurn 14.4× ~2 days Page on-call; begin incident response immediately SlowBurn 6× ~5 days Create P2 ticket; fix before budget exhausts

docs.title: Step 1: Assess Scope (5 minutes)
docs.text:
Open the SLA Executive Dashboard.

Answer these questions: 1. Is this a single service or multiple services? 2. Is the burn rate still rising, stable, or recovering? 3. What is the remaining error budget percentage?

If multiple services are breaching simultaneously → suspect platform (EKS node failure, MSK outage, network). Jump to Platform Triage.

docs.title: Availability breach (5xx rate elevated)
docs.text: Check in order: 1. Recent deployment? Check ArgoCD sync history. If yes → rollback deployment. 2. Kafka consumer lag elevated? Check kafka-overview dashboard. If yes → kafka lag runbook. 3. Database failover in progress? Check RDS event log. If yes → database failover runbook. 4. Pod crash loops? kubectl get pods -n chakra-{service} | grep -v Running. If yes → check logs: kubectl logs -n chakra-{service} deploy/{service}. 5. Upstream external service? Check Payment Gateway status page. If yes → escalate to vendor; update stakeholders.

docs.title: Latency breach (p99 > SLA target)
docs.text: Check in order: 1. Database query regression? Check Grafana slow query panel (Postgres Insights). 2. Redis eviction? Check inventory_projection_lag_seconds metric. If lag > 500ms → Redis memory pressure. 3. JVM / Node GC pressure? Check heap metrics in the service dashboard. 4. Upstream timeout? Check saga duration histogram. If orders_saga_duration_seconds p99 increased → suspect Inventory or Payment Gateway.

docs.title: Step 3: Mitigate
docs.text: Root cause Mitigation Bad deployment rollback-deployment.md DB failover database-failover.md Kafka lag kafka-consumer-lag.md External service outage Enable circuit breaker; update status page; escalate to vendor Pod crash loops Scale down to 1 replica; inspect logs; fix and redeploy Memory pressure Scale horizontally (HPA); check for cardinality explosion in metrics

docs.title: Step 4: Verify Recovery
docs.text:
• Burn rate alert must resolve (no longer firing) for 15 minutes before declaring mitigation complete
  • Check that error_budget_remaining is no longer decreasing
  • If budget < 20% remaining: notify engineering VP; consider feature freeze until budget recovers

docs.title: Step 5: Post-Incident
docs.text:
1. Open a post-incident review (PIR) document within 24 hours
  2. Update the appropriate runbook with any new findings
  3. If the breach was caused by a missing alert or monitoring gap → create a ticket to close the gap before closing the PIR
  4. Update SLA target if the incident revealed the target is unachievable with current architecture

docs.title: Escalation
docs.text: Condition Escalate to Budget < 10% remaining Engineering VP + Domain Lead Multiple services breaching simultaneously Incident Commander; declare P0 Payment Gateway outage > 30 min Vendor SLA claim process (see contracts/slas/orders-sla.yaml exclusions)

# chakraview-ai-dev-model

> A 6-persona framework for human-AI collaborative software development — where humans define correctness and AI agents handle volume.

---

## The Core Claim

Modern software delivery has a bottleneck problem: the people who understand the domain deeply are not bottlenecked on *thinking* — they are bottlenecked on *typing*. AI agents can close that gap, but only if the division of responsibility is explicit and enforced.

This framework operationalises one principle:

> **Humans are accountable for correctness. Agents are accountable for volume.**

Humans author *contracts* — the precise, versioned expression of business intent. Agents implement from those contracts. An agent that drifts from a contract produces a defect traceable to a missing or ambiguous contract, not to the agent itself.

---

## The Six Personas

| # | Persona | Type | Primary output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Human Domain Expert | Human | Contracts, ADR stubs, task specs |
| 2 | Documentation Agent | LLM | Architecture docs, ADRs, runbooks |
| 3 | Script Authoring Agent | LLM (one-shot) | Deterministic scripts in `tooling/` |
| 4 | Script Executor | Automation | Generated artifacts (alerts, Helm, CI) |
| 5 | Service Implementation Agent | LLM | Service source code and tests |
| 6 | Architectural Compliance Agent | LLM (auditor) | Compliance reports |

Full persona definitions: [docs/personas/](docs/personas/index.md)

---

## Repository Map
docs/ model.md The core principle and why it works personas/ All 6 personas — inputs, outputs, constraints mechanism/ Contract boundaries, agent-vs-script decision rule, guardrails workflow/ Complete 7-phase workflow (bootstrap → continuous) task-specs/ How to write task specs; compliance report format case-studies/ Projects built with this model templates/ agent-tasks/ Parameterised task spec templates for LLM agents script-tasks/ Task spec templates for deterministic scripts context/ Context document templates (coding standards, infra, observability) 
---

## See It In Practice

[chakraview-enterprise-modernization](https://github.com/naren-chakraview/chakraview-enterprise-modernization) — modernising a Java EE monolith to cloud-native microservices using all 6 personas.

Read the case study: [How That Project Was Built](https://naren-chakraview.github.io/chakraview-enterprise-modernization/how-this-was-built/)

---
title: Chakraview AI Dev Model
description: A 6-persona framework for human-AI collaborative software development.
---

# Chakraview AI Dev Model

> **Humans are accountable for correctness. Agents are accountable for volume.**

---

## What This Framework Solves

Most AI-assisted development fails at the same place: engineers give agents vague instructions and get inconsistent, unmaintainable output. The root cause is not the agent — it is the missing contract between human intent and agent implementation.

This framework makes that contract explicit. Humans author *contracts* — precise, versioned specifications of business intent. Agents receive task specs that enumerate exactly which contracts to read and exactly what to produce. The line between what humans wrote and what agents built is structural, not informal.

---

## At a Glance

<div class="grid cards" markdown>

-   :material-account-hard-hat:{ .lg .middle } __6 Personas__

    ---

    Human Domain Expert, Documentation Agent, Script Authoring Agent, Script Executor, Implementation Agent, and Compliance Agent — each with explicit inputs, outputs, and constraints.

    [:octicons-arrow-right-24: Browse Personas](personas/index.md)

-   :material-file-document-check:{ .lg .middle } __The Mechanism__

    ---

    Contracts define the boundary. The agent-vs-script decision rule keeps judgment separate from transformation. Guardrails prevent drift.

    [:octicons-arrow-right-24: Understand the Mechanism](mechanism/index.md)

-   :material-source-branch:{ .lg .middle } __7-Phase Workflow__

    ---

    From bootstrap (contracts + validation script) through continuous operation (contract change response, new bounded context). Each phase has explicit persona sequencing and human review gates.

    [:octicons-arrow-right-24: See the Workflow](workflow/index.md)

-   :material-file-code:{ .lg .middle } __Ready-to-Use Templates__

    ---

    Parameterised task spec templates for all agent and script tasks. Drop them in, fill in your domain specifics, and run.

    [:octicons-arrow-right-24: Browse Templates](../templates/)

</div>

---

## The Model in One Diagram
Human authors AI agents build ───────────────────────── ────────────────────────────────── contracts/slas/ → observability/slos/ + alerts/ contracts/event-schemas/ → services//src/domain/events/ contracts/domain-invariants/ → services//src/domain/ (aggregates) docs/adrs/ → services/ + infrastructure/ ai-agents/tasks/agent/ → everything in services/, infrastructure/ tooling/service-manifest.yaml→ tooling/.py / .sh (scripts) 
This is not automation for its own sake. It is a deliberate separation: humans hold accountability for correctness; agents handle volume and consistency.

---

## See It In Practice

[chakraview-enterprise-modernization](https://github.com/naren-chakraview/chakraview-enterprise-modernization) used all 6 personas to modernise a Java EE monolith to cloud-native microservices. Read [how it was built](https://naren-chakraview.github.io/chakraview-enterprise-modernization/how-this-was-built/).
